meta.title: Quote vs Estimate vs Invoice: Which One Should You Use?
meta.description: Don't confuse your clients. Learn the legal and practical differences between a quote, an estimate, and an invoice, and when to use each in your sales process.

blocks.title: Quote vs Estimate vs Invoice
blocks.subtitle: Navigating the sales cycle requires sending the right document at the right time. Learn exactly when to issue an estimate, lock in a quote, and finally send an invoice.

blocks.authorBio: Jane is a certified CPA with over 15 years of experience in small business accounting.

blocks.heading: The Evolution of a Sale
blocks.text: The sales process is an evolution from an educated guess to a legally binding demand for payment. If you send an invoice when a client was expecting an estimate, you risk appearing aggressive and losing the deal. If you send an estimate when you should have sent a quote, you leave yourself open to legal disputes over final pricing. Understanding this progression is vital for healthy client relationships.

blocks.title: The Three Stages of Billing
rows: Fixed price (cannot change without a new agreement)
After work is completed (or at milestones)
Final price based on the accepted quote

blocks.definition: An educated guess of how much a job will cost based on initial information. It is subject to change as the scope of work becomes clearer.
A formal, fixed price offer for a specific scope of work. Once the client accepts a quote, the price cannot legally change unless the scope changes.

events.title: Rough Guess
events.description: Client asks 'roughly how much?' You provide an Estimate based on past similar projects.

events.title: Fixed Offer
events.description: You visit the site, measure the exact dimensions, and provide a fixed Quote valid for 30 days.

events.title: Execution
events.description: The client accepts the Quote. You perform the work exactly as described.

events.title: Payment Demand
events.description: Work is done. You issue an Invoice matching the Quote to collect your money.

blocks.tip: Always put an expiration date on your Quotes (e.g., 'Valid for 30 Days'). Material costs fluctuate, and you don't want to be legally bound to a price you quoted six months ago.

mistakes.mistake: Treating a quote like an estimate.
mistakes.solution: If you issue a 'Quote' for $1,000, but the job ends up taking longer, you cannot simply invoice the client for $1,500. A quote is fixed. If you want flexibility, issue an Estimate.

blocks.heading: Ready to send a document?
blocks.subtext: You can change the title of our free generator to 'Quote', 'Estimate', or 'Invoice' with a single click.
